series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 01/10] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching the next request from sending it, making the control flow clearer and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. It removes duplicated code in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() and introduces a new function fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req() to fetch the next request. The send operation is now handled by a separate function fuse_uring_send(). This change simplifies the logic for handling requests and improves maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 02/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers to a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts the header copying into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(), which centralizes error handling and rate-limited logging, making the code more clear and maintainable. The patch modifies several functions that interact with the ring, replacing direct calls to copy_to_user() with calls to the new function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 03/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the fuse kernel module. It extracts this logic into a new function called copy_header_from_ring(), which makes it clearer and more centralized, and also handles errors and rate-limited logging. This change is part of a larger series of patches to improve the io-uring functionality in the fuse module.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 04/10] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This change will simplify both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying when buffer rings are added, making it easier to manage and maintain the code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 05/10] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up copy state in the FUSE implementation of dev_uring by introducing a new helper function `setup_fuse_copy_state`. This function encapsulates the logic for importing user buffers and initializing the copy state. The existing code is simplified and made more modular, reducing duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 06/10] fuse: use named constants for io-uring iovec indices
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces magic indices 0 and 1 in the iovec array with named constants FUSE_URING_IOV_HEADERS and FUSE_URING_IOV_PAYLOAD, making the code self-documenting and preparing for future buffer ring support. The patch modifies several functions in the fuse module to use these new constants instead of hardcoded indices. This change improves code readability and maintainability by eliminating magic numbers and making it easier to understand the purpose of each index.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring)
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer pools in the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass a contiguous allocation of buffers that can be used on demand by the kernel. This decouples the number of entries from the number of payload buffers required, reducing memory usage and enabling future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through an iovec payload in the first sqe. The kernel manages buffer selection and recycling using a buffer ring data structure.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 08/10] fuse: support registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for using registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring, allowing servers to use the same pinned pages for subsequent I/O operations and reducing overhead. Registered buffers require enabling the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag and setting the FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS init flag during registration. The patch also includes benchmark results showing a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads and other improvements for different types of reads.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 09/10] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds zero-copy support to the fuse io-uring implementation in the Linux kernel. It eliminates memory copies between userspace, the kernel, and the fuse server for read/write operations by allowing the server to directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer. The patch requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS capabilities and is gated behind these requirements. The zero-copy write path involves registering relevant pages in the server's io-uring registered buffer table, operating on these pages using fixed buffer operations, and unregistering them when the request completes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 10/10] docs: fuse: add io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy documentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module for using buffer pools and zero-copy functionality with io-uring. The documentation explains how to use buffer pools, which decouple the relationship between entries and payload buffers, allowing for more efficient memory usage. It also describes how the server manages the buffer pool through a buffer ring, enabling features like incremental buffer consumption and multiple buffer sizes per ring. This patch aims to improve performance by reducing unnecessary copies of data between kernel and user space.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer pools and zero-copy functionality to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing servers to pass in a managed buffer pool that reduces memory usage requirements and enables future optimizations. It also allows for direct access to client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer, requiring CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges. The changes are based on registered buffers and zero-copy, which can provide significant throughput improvements depending on the server's per-request latency and backing I/O characteristics.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about duplicated logic in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() and fuse_uring_send(). They simplified the logic by removing duplicate code from fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring(), decoupling request fetching from the send operation, and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. The patch removes 50 lines of code and adds 28 new lines.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the tight coupling between ents and buffers in fuse io-uring by explaining their new buffer pool design, which decouples them and allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses per i/o request, explaining that registered buffers eliminate this overhead by pinning pages upfront. The author provided details on how to use registered buffers, including setting flags in the registration sqe and following standard protocol for using registered buffers.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the zero-copy implementation's complexity and overhead, explaining that it requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS to prevent partially-optimized modes. The author provided detailed request flow diagrams for both write and read paths, highlighting how pages are registered and unregistered in the io_uring registered buffer table.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Bernd's concern about registering ring entries without payloads, explaining that entries may be used for servicing requests even if there are less buffers than the number of entries, and that it would allow multiple entries to use non-overlapping regions of the same buffer concurrently. The author also mentioned that supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring and some requests not needing payload buffers justify decoupling entries from buffers.

> > either for the non-zero-copyable args or if the request's args aren't
> > page-backed. Unfortunately I think many servers may not be able to use
> > zero-copy due to the CAP_SYS_ADMIN requirements, so I think it'd be
> > ideal to minimize the memory consumption needed for the non-zero-copy
> > path, through the buffer pool for the payload buffers.
>
> Well, my problem is still why would we need to register ring entries if
> they don't have buffers - why not just register less ring entries then.

Entries may be used for servicing requests even if there are less
buffers than the number of entries. Most requests don't use the full
payload buffer size, so for example with incremental buffer
consumption, this would allow multiple entries to use non-overlapping
regions of the same buffer concurrently. As well, if we want to
support multiple buffer sizes per ring, there shouldn't be a 1:1
mapping between ents and buffers. There's also some requests that
don't need to use payload buffers at all (eg flush, fsync, rmdir,
etc).  As well for registered buffers / buffer pinning, it is more
optimal for the buffer pool to be allocated contiguously and pinned
contiguously rather than passed in one by one as a 1:1 entry:buffer
mapping.

Thanks,
Joanne

I don't think this is a good idea. The server would have to
guesstimate this as it doesn't know the request size until it sees the
request. The server could speculatively pre-register ring ents ahead
of time but there's no guarantee that would be the right size for the
ring entry either. And once added, if the workload pattern changes,
then the ent and allocated buffer may not be useful anymore. This also
adds (imo needless) complexity to the kernel side. Additionally, the
dynamic ring ents would in the ideal case have payload buffers pinned,
which there is not an non-messy way to do (for buffer pools, the pool
buffer pinning goes through io-uring registered buffers infrastructure
prior to being passed to fuse at registration time). Imo the better
way if you wanted to dynamically add ents would be to add a new buffer
pool to the ring.

Please take a look at patch 8 ([PATCH v3 08/10] fuse: support
registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring) [1]. The buffer pool
is contiguously allocated and registered as an io-uring registered
buffer, and then passed to fuse and by that point, the buffers have
already been pinned by io-uring.

Even irrespective of page pinning, it would still be good to pass the
buffers contiguously up front as a pool instead of per-ent, as the
purpose of this series is to decouple the 1:1 entry:buffer mapping. Eg
if the request an ent is servicing does not require buffers, a buffer
should not be reserved for that ent, amongst other benefits of a
decoupled mapping.

That is orthogonal to this series. This series focuses on payload
buffers, and header pinning is not part of this series as
bufpools/bufrings do not need pinned headers, as it makes no

I implemented header pinning as part of that, as that is when header

>
>
> Maybe let's summarize
>
> Bufrings
> - Fixed size, allocated at startup, cannot be easily increased or decreased
> - Allows 0 size payloads (FUSE requests that only need the header)
>
> Entries with payload
> - Allows more flexible setup - fuse server _could_ (not implemented as

>

>

I think perhaps there is a misunderstanding of the patches in this series.

This series implements buffer pools, which decouple the 1:1 mapping
between ents and buffers that currently exists. Right now, ents and
buffers are tightly coupled where each entry has its own dedicated
payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where each buffer
must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size.

This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool where the buffers
are not statically mapped to an ent at registration time. This
decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use
fuse-io-uring. It is the necessary stepping stone for future
optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption (allowing
multiple entries to use non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
concurrently) and supporting multi-size buffers per ring.

Thanks,
Joanne

It's not just about the memory savings. Fundamentally, I believe it's
the wrong abstraction to statically couple ents to buffers 1:1 as a
fixed mapping. Entries and buffers serve different purposes. Entries
handle request flow, buffers hold data. Statically coupling them 1:1
is inflexible, introduces unnecessary complexity, and imo has no added
benefit. This series decouples these two concepts. It allows
dynamically adding ents in the future as *generic* ents not tied to a
particular buffer size (eg the added ent can be used to service *any*
request size, not just the size of the buffer it's tied to). With
tying ents to buffers, the server is stuck with a static distribution
that can't adapt to workload changes.

I think we're talking past each other. Buffer pools aren't about "zero

entries in general. Zero-payload requests are one beneficiary, but the
design enables incremental consumption and multi-size buffers as
future work precisely because of this decoupling.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing feedback on the buffer allocation mechanism in fuse io-uring, specifically the issue that most request types require less than the maximum payload size, leading to inefficient memory usage. The author proposes decoupling entries from payload buffers and using a pool of dynamically selected buffers instead. They are seeking input on this approach.

> has its own dedicated payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where
> each buffer must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size. This
> is suboptimal as most request types require vastly less bytes than the maximum
> payload size and some requests do not require payload buffers at all.
>
> This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool (internally managed as a
> buffer ring) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. This
> decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use fuse-io-uring.
> It allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption and
> supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring.
>
> This series also allows servers to use io-uring registered buffers as the
> buffer pool which eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and
> translating virtual addreses per i/o request.
>
> This series additionally adds zero copy to fuse io-uring. The server can
> directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through
> an intermediary buffer. This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and using
> registered buffers.
>
> This series has a dependency on io-uring registered bvec buffers changes
> in [1].
>
> The throughput improvements from registered buffers and zero-copy depends on
> how much of the server's per-request latency is spent on data copying vs
> backing I/O. When backing I/O dominates, the saved memcpy is a negligible
> fraction of overall latency. Please also note that for the server to
> read/write into the zero-copied pages, the read/write must go through io-uring
> as an IORING_OP_READ_FIXED / IORING_OP_WRITE_FIXED operation. If the
> server's backing I/O is instantaneous (eg served from cache), the
> overhead of the additional io_uring operation may negate the savings
> from eliminating the memcpy.
>
> In benchmarks using passthrough_hp on a high-performance NVMe-backed
> system, using registered buffers showed around a 10% throughput improvement
> for direct randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2400 MiB/s), a 4% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2620 MiB/s), a 8% improvement for buffered
> randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2280 MiB/s), and a 6% improvement for buffered
> sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2670 MiB/s).
>
> Zero-copy showed around a 35% throughput improvement for direct
> randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for
> buffered randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2470 MiB/s), and a 10% improvement
> for buffered sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2750 MiB/s). I didn't see
> enough of a clear improvement for writes due to write latency being I/O
> dominated.
>
> The benchmarks were run using:
>   fio --name=test_run --ioengine=sync --rw=rand{read,write} --bs=1M
>   --size=1G --numjobs=2 --ramp_time=30 --group_reporting=1
>
> The libfuse changes can be found in [2]. To test the server, run:
> sudo ~/libfuse/build/example/passthrough_hp ~/src ~/mounts/tmp
> --nopassthrough -o io_uring_zero_copy -o io_uring_q_depth=8
> Once this series is merged, the libfuse changes will be tidied up and
> submitted upstream.
>
> Thanks,
> Joanne
>
> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260402160929.2749744-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com/T/#t
> [2] https://github.com/joannekoong/libfuse/commits/zero_copy_v3/
>

Miklos,

This series has stalled on an architectural question and I'd
appreciate your input on the direction.

Background:
In fuse io-uring, entries are reusable communication slots between the
kernel and the daemon. When a request needs to be sent from the kernel
to the daemon, it is assigned to an available entry. The entry carries
the request to userspace, and the response comes back through the same
entry. The number of entries determines how many requests can be
serviced concurrently. Currently, when the daemon registers an entry
during fuse io-uring setup, it passes in a payload buffer (sized to
the maximum payload, typically 1 MiB) alongside it. The buffer is tied
to the entry for the lifetime of the connection.

Problem that needs to be solved:
Most request types (lookup, getattr, open, etc) require far less than
the maximum payload size, and some (flush, fsync, etc) require no
payload buffer at all. Every entry reserves a full 1 MiB buffer
regardless of actual need. On systems with many entries, this memory
cost scales linearly - for example, 64 entries across 8 queues
requires 512 MiB of payload buffers alone.

Approach A:
Decouple entries from payload buffers. Entries become generic - any
entry can use any buffer from the pool. At setup time, the daemon
passes in a contiguous pool of memory, and the kernel dynamically
selects and recycles buffers from this pool as entries are assigned
requests. Entries servicing requests with no payloads consume no
buffer, and the pool can have fewer buffers than entries. This
supports adding multi-size buffers and incremental buffer consumption
(multiple entries using non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
concurrently) as optimizations in the future.

Approach B:
Keep the 1:1 entry-buffer coupling, but allow entries to be registered
with different payload buffer sizes at setup time. The daemon would
register entries with smaller buffers for metadata-heavy workloads and
larger buffers for data-heavy ones. This keeps the existing model of
passing in a payload buffer during entry registration at setup time
and requires no uapi additions. Each entry registration adds buffer
capacity whereas with buffer pools, buffer capacity would have to be
added as a separate pool.

I want to make sure I'm representing both approaches fairly - Bernd,
please correct anything I've gotten wrong.

Miklos, what are your thoughts?

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.summary: Bernd Schubert questioned whether header pinning is still possible, noting that if it's not done, io_uring_cmd_complete_in_task() cannot be avoided, resulting in a context switch for sync requests. He also proposed an alternative approach where the payload would be registered per ring entry and only the header would become a buffer ring.

short question, I didn't check the code is header pinning still
*possible*? The problem if we do not pin headers is that
io_uring_cmd_complete_in_task() cannot be avoided, as the application
cannot write into fuse-server buffers and headers without pre-pinned
pages. And io_uring_cmd_complete_in_task() basically means a context
switch for all sync requests.

libfuse sync-init (remaining are bugs), Darricks series is also almost
ready. Although I don't promise this weekend - reserved for Darrick and
other libfuse changes.

One piece I had been pondering a bit about, though did not dive into the
code yet.
What is if payload would be registered per ring entry (as without your
changes) and just the header would become a buffer ring (so that could
be pinned that way without loosing one page per entry)?  For zero-copy
we could register entries without a payload? Advantage here would be
that that header buffer would determine max number of entries, only
little memory would be needed for over-subscribed entries, but entries
themselves could be registered dynamically on demand.


Thanks,
Bernd

>> The libfuse changes can be found in [2]. To test the server, run:
>> sudo ~/libfuse/build/example/passthrough_hp ~/src ~/mounts/tmp
>> --nopassthrough -o io_uring_zero_copy -o io_uring_q_depth=8
>> Once this series is merged, the libfuse changes will be tidied up and
>> submitted upstream.
>>
>> Thanks,
>> Joanne
>>
>> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260402160929.2749744-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com/T/#t
>> [2] https://github.com/joannekoong/libfuse/commits/zero_copy_v3/
>>
> 
> Miklos,
> 
> This series has stalled on an architectural question and I'd
> appreciate your input on the direction.
> 
> Background:
> In fuse io-uring, entries are reusable communication slots between the
> kernel and the daemon. When a request needs to be sent from the kernel
> to the daemon, it is assigned to an available entry. The entry carries
> the request to userspace, and the response comes back through the same
> entry. The number of entries determines how many requests can be
> serviced concurrently. Currently, when the daemon registers an entry
> during fuse io-uring setup, it passes in a payload buffer (sized to
> the maximum payload, typically 1 MiB) alongside it. The buffer is tied
> to the entry for the lifetime of the connection.
> 
> Problem that needs to be solved:
> Most request types (lookup, getattr, open, etc) require far less than
> the maximum payload size, and some (flush, fsync, etc) require no
> payload buffer at all. Every entry reserves a full 1 MiB buffer
> regardless of actual need. On systems with many entries, this memory
> cost scales linearly - for example, 64 entries across 8 queues
> requires 512 MiB of payload buffers alone.
> 
> Approach A:
> Decouple entries from payload buffers. Entries become generic - any
> entry can use any buffer from the pool. At setup time, the daemon
> passes in a contiguous pool of memory, and the kernel dynamically
> selects and recycles buffers from this pool as entries are assigned
> requests. Entries servicing requests with no payloads consume no
> buffer, and the pool can have fewer buffers than entries. This
> supports adding multi-size buffers and incremental buffer consumption
> (multiple entries using non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
> concurrently) as optimizations in the future.
> 
> Approach B:
> Keep the 1:1 entry-buffer coupling, but allow entries to be registered
> with different payload buffer sizes at setup time. The daemon would
> register entries with smaller buffers for metadata-heavy workloads and
> larger buffers for data-heavy ones. This keeps the existing model of
> passing in a payload buffer during entry registration at setup time
> and requires no uapi additions. Each entry registration adds buffer
> capacity whereas with buffer pools, buffer capacity would have to be
> added as a separate pool.
> 
> I want to make sure I'm representing both approaches fairly - Bernd,
> please correct anything I've gotten wrong.

I think for B "at setup time" is not entirely correct - buffers in the
current model buffers and entries can be registered at run time.


I have been thinking about approach C, which I think is in between A and B.
I my mind approach C does the registration per entry, but payload
buffers get added to a buf array, if a payload is set.
Max array size would be negotiated as in your approach or maybe better
with a config SQE. Different IO size could achieved by multiple buf arrays.


Thanks,
Bernd

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module. The current implementation reserves a full 1 MiB payload buffer for each entry, which can lead to significant memory waste on systems with many entries. Two approaches are proposed: decoupling entries from payload buffers by using a shared pool of dynamically selected and recycled buffers, or allowing entries to be registered with different payload buffer sizes at setup time. The goal is to reduce memory usage and improve performance for FUSE-based file systems.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm: list_lru: fix set_shrinker_bit() call during race with cgroup deletion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a long-standing bug in the list_lru code, where the shrinker bit is set on the wrong cgroup during a race condition between list_lru_add() and cgroup deletion. The fix involves returning the 'effective' memcg from the locking function and having the caller use that to set the shrinker bit.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function, which is used to acquire a lock for list additions and deletions. The function will not fail to acquire the lock, so these checks are no longer needed. This change simplifies the code and makes it more efficient.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the unlock_list_lru() function by consolidating its implementation into a single inline function, eliminating duplicate code in both MEMCG and !MEMCG variants. The function is responsible for unlocking the lock associated with a list_lru_one structure, taking into account whether interrupts are disabled or not. This change simplifies the codebase and reduces maintenance overhead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for a race condition between list LRU operations and cgroup deletion to the caller of lock_list_lru_of_memcg(). This is done by removing the dead list check from the function itself, allowing for future unification of the lock_list_lru() variants. The change affects only the MEMCG variant of lock_list_lru(), which needs to handle this specific race condition.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API. It provides normal and irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the addition and deletion functions to allow callers to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state. This is necessary because some users, such as the THP shrinker, require this synchronization to maintain consistency. The patch adds new locking functions and modifies existing ones to support this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function called folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() that allocates list_lru heads for shrinkable folios under the memory cgroup (memcg) they are associated with. The function is designed to handle the complexities of accessing the memcg of a folio, which requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. It aims to make the existence check lightweight under RCU and only pin the memcg when necessary for allocation. This patch is part of a larger series that prepares the THP shrinker to use list_lru, and it has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] mm: memory: flatten alloc_anon_folio() retry loop
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the alloc_anon_folio() function by flattening its retry loop to improve code readability and maintainability. The original top-level if statement was buried four levels deep, making it difficult to understand and modify. By moving the success path to a more visible location, the code becomes cleaner and easier to work with. This change is made in preparation for additional code that will be added in the next patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change addresses a suboptimal handling of cgroups in the deferred split queue, where large pages on other nodes were being split unnecessarily. The new approach streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers already. The patch also adds calls to create node heads for the cgroup at once when splittable pages are created, eliminating the need for special care in migration code between nodes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series, version 5, aims to switch the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of the current implementation. The changes include fixing a long-standing issue in set_shrinker_bit() where the bit can be set on a dying child memcg instead of the ancestor that received the item. Other changes include adapting to the new lock_list_lru_of_memcg() signature, propagating folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() failure, and fixing a use-after-free bug in deferred_split_isolate(). The patches have been rebased onto the latest mm-unstable tree.

Reviewer SeongJae Park noted that the patch causes a build failure in UM mode due to an implicit function declaration error for folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is unset, and suggested defining the function for this case.

SeongJae Park noted that without the definition of folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() for !CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE, UM mode kunit fails due to an implicit function declaration error in __swap_cache_alloc(), and provided a patch to define this function.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm: don't allow empty relative nodemask in mpol_relative_nodemask()
Re: [PATCH] mm: don't allow empty relative nodemask in mpol_relative_nodemask()
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch prevents empty relative nodemasks in mpol_relative_nodemask() by adding a check for the node weight being zero, which is not sufficient as it allows empty nodemasks for certain policy modes. Instead, the full policy-nodemask checking section from mpol_new() should be called from mpol_set_nodemask(). This ensures that all places where mpol_relative_nodemask() is called perform the same validation as mpol_new().

review_comments.author: Yury Norov
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Yury Norov noted that nodes_fold() cannot handle an empty bitmap (sz == 0), and proposed two options to address this issue: disallowing empty relmaps in mpol_relative_nodemask() or following the behavior of nodes_onto() by clearing the ret mask and bailing out when an empty relmask is encountered.

reviewer questioned the approach of checking for empty relative nodemask in mpol_relative_nodemask(), pointing out that MPOL_F_RELATIVE_NODES is set only when called, and that rebind case is not handled correctly
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joshua.

Indeed, quick and dirty shot.

The problem is that nodes_fold() can't work with the sz == 0. In
other words, folding to a 0-bit bitmap is an error. We don't check
that on bitmaps level because it's an internal helper, and it's a
caller's responsibility to validate the parameters.

nodes_onto(), or more specifically bitmap_onto(), is a different
story. In case of empty relmap, the function actually clears all the
bits in dst and returns.

I see 2 options to move this forward.

1. Simply disallow empty relmap in mpol_relative_nodemask(). There's
no valid cases for it, AFAIK, so the nodes_fold() limitation looks
reasonable. We can consider it as a new policy.

We've got 2 users for mpol_relative_nodemask(). In mpol_set_nodemask()
we can simply propagate the error; and in mpol_rebind_nodemask() we
can throw a warning and do nothing.

2. Follow the spirit of the nodes_onto(), and in case of empty
relmask, clean the ret mask and bail out

I'm in a favor for the 1st option, because empty relmask looks buggy
anyways.

---

Not sure I understand this. The mpol_relative_nodemask() is called
only if MPOL_F_RELATIVE_NODES is set. In mpol_rebind_nodemask(), if
both MPOL_F_STATIC_NODES and MPOL_F_RELATIVE_NODES are set, the former
wins. How would the RELATIVE mode mess with the others?

The mpol_new() code seemingly tries to disable empty nodes in case of
MPOL_DEFAILT and MPOL_PREFERRED + MPOL_F_RELATIVE_NODES, but obviously
it doesn't work very well in the rebind case.

Anyways, I'm not really deep in mempolicy domain, so please educate me if
I miss something.

Thanks,
Yury

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that an empty nodemask should be checked for in mpol_relative_nodemask(), but disagreed with the proposed solution of checking if node weight is 0, instead suggesting to restructure and call the full policy-nodemask checking section from mpol_new().

Author acknowledges that mpol_set_nodemask should never be called with an empty nodemask if MPOL_F_RELATIVE_NODES is set, and suggests additional checks in mpol_rebind_policy to catch other potential failures.

Author acknowledged a mistake in their original patch, specifically that they missed the case where MPOL_DEFAULT and MPOL_PREFERRED allow empty nodemasks when !STATIC && !RELATIVE, and agreed to restructure the code to fix this issue.

Author acknowledges that the proposed patch approach won't work for MPOL_DEFAULT and MPOL_PREFERRED cases, agrees that mpol_new's policy-nodemask checking section should be called from mpol_set_nodemask as well.
sentiment_signals: acknowledges a need for extra defensive checking
review_comments.raw_body:
I see, thank you for helping me understand. Yeah, we probably don't want
an empty nodemask here regardless of policy, as long as MPOL_F_RELATIVE_NODES
is set.

---

I think we should never be able to reach mpol_set_nodemask with an empty
nodemask if MPOL_F_RELATIVE_NODES is set. Not sure if we need to be extra
defensive here.

For mpol_rebind_nodemask I think we should actually do some more checks,
I think we should do it in mpol_rebind_policy since it gives us an opportunity
to catch other sources of failure too, like calling mpol_rebind_preferred 
with an empty nodemask as well (which shouldn't be allowed for MPOL_F_{
RELATIVE, STATIC}_NODES) as far as I can tell from the checks in mpol_new.

Setting empty nodemask for mpol_rebind_preferred won't throw a div0 error
like for mpol_rebind_nodemask but we can at least throw a warning like you
suggested. 

Does that make sense? This is your fix and if you would prefer to address only
the div0 case, that makes sense too, since the empty nodemask for preferred
is more of a semantic incorrectness and will not cause panics.
Entirely up to you! : -)

---

Yes, you're right, the case that MPOL_DEFAULT and MPOL_PREFERRED allows empty
nodemasks is precisely when !STATIC && !RELATIVE :p this is my bad for missing
that case completely.

---

Thank you, I have also learned a lot looking into this to think about what the
best solution is!
Joshua

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 01/15] mm: decouple protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the protnone helpers (pte_protnone and pmd_protnone) from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING, making them available independently of NUMA balancing. The capability to detect present-but-inaccessible page table entries is useful beyond NUMA balancing and is now enabled on six architectures that support it. The patch introduces a new configuration option, CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE, which depends on the presence of protnone PTEs in an architecture's code. This change does not introduce any functional changes but rather makes the infrastructure available for other uses.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 02/15] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE bit macros to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the macros related to the uffd-wp PTE bit in various architectures, including x86 and arm64, to simply 'uffd' to reflect that it is used for userfaultfd tracking rather than write protection. The rename is purely mechanical and does not change any behavior. This is done in preparation for a second consumer of this bit, which will use it for access-tracking PTEs. The patch updates the macros in several header files across different architectures.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 03/15] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE accessors to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames kernel-internal functions and macros related to the uffd-wp PTE bit, which is used for userfaultfd write protection. The renaming is done to reflect that the bit's meaning depends on the VMA flag (WP or RWP), not its name. This change does not affect behavior but makes the code more consistent and easier to understand. The patch touches various files in the Linux kernel source tree, including architecture-specific header files, mm modules, and userfaultfd-related code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 04/15] userfaultfd: test uffd VMA flags through the vma_flags_t API
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the userfaultfd VMA flag helpers to use the vma_flags_t accessor, which is a more modern and unified API for accessing VMA flags. The change is made to accommodate config-gated per-mode masks, ensuring that the code remains compatible with different build configurations. The updated code uses the vma_test_any_mask() function to check for specific UFFD flags, replacing direct access to vm_flags. This change does not introduce any functional differences, as the same bits are being read and the masks fold to the same code as before.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 05/15] mm: add VM_UFFD_RWP VMA flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new VMA flag, VM_UFFD_RWP, to support userfaultfd read-write protection (RWP). RWP extends the existing write-protection mechanism to also trap read accesses through userfaultfd. The patch introduces a new flag and related helpers, but does not yet set or query the flag. It is part of a larger series that will implement full RWP functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 06/15] mm: add MM_CP_UFFD_RWP change_protection() flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new flags to the change_protection() function: MM_CP_UFFD_RWP and MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE. The first flag installs a PAGE_NONE PTE with the uffd bit set, making it distinguishable from other faults. The second flag restores the original vma->vm_page_prot and clears the uffd bit. These flags are used by userfaultfd RWP to protect pages from being accessed, and they share the same PTE bit as MM_CP_UFFD_WP. The patch updates several kernel files, including mm/huge_memory.c, mm/hugetlb.c, and mm/mprotect.c, to support these new flags.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 07/15] mm: preserve RWP marker across PTE rewrites
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that the RWP (Read-Only Write-Protection) marker is preserved across PTE rewrites in various kernel paths, including swap, device-exclusive, migration, fork, and mprotect operations. Without this preservation, the RWP tracking would be silently dropped, leading to potential security vulnerabilities. The patch modifies several kernel functions to restore the PAGE_NONE semantics when necessary, thereby maintaining the integrity of the RWP marker.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 08/15] mm: handle VM_UFFD_RWP in khugepaged, rmap, and GUP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends three Linux kernel paths to treat VM_UFFD_RWP (User Faultfd Read-Write Protected) the same as VM_UFFD_WP (User Faultfd Write Protected). The affected paths are khugepaged, rmap, and GUP. In khugepaged, ranges with VM_UFFD_RWP markers are protected from collapse. In rmap, unmap batching is capped at 1 for VM_UFFD_RWP to ensure each PTE can be handled individually. In GUP, faults are forced on VM_UFFD_RWP VMAs regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT, as RWP uses protnone as an access-tracking marker rather than a NUMA hinting marker.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 09/15] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT plumbing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to userfaultfd, allowing userspace to track read-write protection (RWP) on memory ranges. It introduces two new ioctls: UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT, which enable RWP tracking and allow installing or removing RWP on registered ranges. The patch also adds a capability bit, UFFD_FEATURE_RWP, to control access to this feature. The registration of RWP sets the VM_UFFD_RWP flag on the VMA, and re-registering a range without dropping existing per-PTE markers is prevented. This patch lays the groundwork for exposing the UAPI in a follow-up patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 10/15] mm/userfaultfd: add RWP fault delivery and expose UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for read-write protection (RWP) in the userfaultfd interface, allowing userspace to track and manage RWP-protected pages. It introduces a new fault delivery mechanism that distinguishes between RWP faults and other types of faults. The patch also exposes UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and related API calls to userspace. The implementation works with anonymous, shmem, and hugetlb memory types. The goal is to provide a more fine-grained control over page access permissions in the presence of userfaultfd.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 11/15] mm/pagemap: add PAGE_IS_ACCESSED for RWP tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new category to the pagemap, called PAGE_IS_ACCESSED, which tracks read-write protection (RWP) in userfaultfd (UFFD). It introduces a new bit in the PTE that is used to indicate whether a page has been accessed under RWP. The patch also updates the documentation and code to reflect this change, ensuring that UFFDIO_WRITEPROTECT and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT are properly distinguished. This change allows for more precise tracking of page access patterns and improves the accuracy of pagemap information.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 12/15] userfaultfd: add UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC for async fault resolution
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the userfaultfd system called UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC, which allows for asynchronous resolution of read-write page faults. This is useful for working-set tracking in virtual machines where the kernel can restore access to pages without blocking the faulting thread. The VMM can then reconstruct the access pattern after the fact using PAGEMAP_SCAN. The patch also includes changes to handle writable private anonymous PTEs and upgrade them when necessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 13/15] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_SET_MODE for runtime sync/async toggle
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl, UFFDIO_SET_MODE, to toggle the async mode of userfaultfd at runtime without re-registering the userfaultfd. This allows virtual machine monitors (VMMs) to switch between sync and async read-write protection (RWP) modes on-the-fly for tasks such as working set scanning and page eviction. The ioctl takes an enable/disable bitmask of UFFD_FEATURE_* flags, with only RWP_ASYNC toggleable today. When switching to async mode, pending sync waiters are woken up so they can retry and auto-resolve under the new mode. This patch also includes changes to userfaultfd_show_fdinfo() to handle the lockless observer case.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 14/15] selftests/mm: add userfaultfd RWP tests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds userfaultfd (UFFD) read-write-protect (RWP) tests to the Linux kernel selftests suite. The tests cover various scenarios, including asynchronous and synchronous modes, page mapping, memory protection, and fork operations. They verify that RWP works correctly in different types of memory, such as anonymous, shared, and huge pages. The patch also includes test cases for edge cases like flipping between sync and async modes without re-registering the UFFD and closing the UFFD to restore page permissions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 15/15] Documentation/userfaultfd: document RWP working set tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's admin guide on userfaultfd (UFFD), specifically covering read-write protection (RWP) working set tracking. It explains the sync and async fault models, UFFDIO_RWPROTECT semantics, and UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC. The documentation also covers a typical VMM working-set-tracking workflow from detection to eviction and back to async mode. This patch includes an example of how RWP works and its differences compared to other memory operations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds userfaultfd support for tracking the working set of VM guest memory, enabling a VMM to identify cold pages and evict them to tiered or remote storage. The approach introduces a new userfaultfd registration mode (MODE_RWP) that uses PAGE_NONE on PTEs to mark pages as inaccessible while keeping them resident. This allows the kernel to deliver UFFD_PAGEFAULT_FLAG_RWP messages to registered handlers, which can resolve faults with UFFDIO_RWPROTECT.

review_comments.author: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft)
review_comments.summary: Approved the addition of VM_UFFD_RWP to VM_COPY_ON_FORK and the preservation of RWP markers across PTE rewrites. However, suggested that the patch series should be rebased on top of a more recent version of the userfaultfd/pagemap: pre-existing fixes series.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)
review_comments.summary: Responded to Mike's comments, stating that the patch series will be rebased on top of a more recent version of the userfaultfd/pagemap: pre-existing fixes series. Also mentioned that the addition of VM_UFFD_RWP to VM_COPY_ON_FORK and the preservation of RWP markers across PTE rewrites are essential for the correct functioning of the new registration mode.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/6] userfaultfd/pagemap: pre-existing fixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/6] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix make_uffd_wp_huge_pte() prot-update race
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a race condition in the make_uffd_wp_huge_pte() function, which is responsible for setting the UFFD_WP bit on present HugeTLB PTEs. The issue arises when the function fetches a snapshot of the PTE without first clearing it with huge_ptep_modify_prot_start(), allowing hardware updates to occur between the original read and commit. To fix this, the patch mirrors the pattern used in make_uffd_wp_pte() for non-hugetlb PTEs by adding calls to huge_ptep_modify_prot_start() and ptep_modify_prot_commit(). This ensures that the kernel-owned snapshot remains consistent until the commit, preventing the loss of updates. The migration case is left unchanged as it does not involve present PTEs.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/6] fs/proc/task_mmu: use huge_page_size() in pagemap_scan_hugetlb_entry()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the pagemap_scan_hugetlb_entry function where it incorrectly checks for partial huge pages by comparing against HPAGE_SIZE, which is incorrect for gigantic hugetlb hstates. The correct approach is to compare against the actual page size of the hstate. This fix ensures that the walker does not prematurely abort the write protection (WP) check.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/6] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix hugetlb self-deadlock in pagemap_scan_pte_hole()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a self-deadlock issue in the Linux kernel's procfs task_mmu module when handling huge page (hugetlb) memory mappings. The deadlock occurs because the pagemap_scan_pte_hole function, which is responsible for scanning and write-protecting unpopulated hugetlb entries, takes a read lock on the hugetlb VMA while also trying to take a write lock on the same VMA. This patch avoids this deadlock by directly installing uffd-wp markers under the page-table lock, rather than routing through the uffd_wp_range function. The fix is similar to how populated hugetlb entries are handled in pagemap_scan_hugetlb_entry. The patch also ensures that PMD sharing is disabled on uffd-wp VMAs, making it safe to skip taking the VMA write lock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/6] mm/huge_memory: preserve pmd_swp_uffd_wp on device-private PMD downgrade
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the huge_memory module where a device-private PMD swap entry's UFFD_WP bit was being lost during a downgrade operation, allowing mprotect() to bypass the trap on swap-in. The fix is achieved by carrying the pmd_swp_uffd_wp flag across when rewriting a writable device-private PMD swap entry into a readable one. This matches the behavior of copy_huge_pmd() in the fork path and ensures that the UFFD_WP bit is preserved.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/6] userfaultfd: gate must_wait writability check on pte_present()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the userfaultfd code to gate a writability check on pte_present(). The check is intended to determine if a fault still requires userspace to handle it, and was previously being performed without taking the page table lock. However, this could lead to incorrect results when dealing with swap or migration entries, which share the same position as present PTE bits. By gating the writability check on pte_present(), the code ensures that only present PTEs are inspected, preventing incorrect results and allowing the fault path to retry if necessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/6] userfaultfd: build __VMA_UFFD_FLAGS from config-gated masks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue where the userfaultfd VMA flags bitmap was being written out of bounds on 32-bit architectures. The problem occurred because the bit numbers used in __VMA_UFFD_FLAGS were not valid for all architectures, leading to silent corruption of the mask. To fix this, the patch introduces a new function mk_vma_flags_from_masks() that builds the mask from whole per-mode masks that collapse to EMPTY_VMA_FLAGS when their feature is unavailable. This approach ensures that out-of-range bits are never materialized and the in-range fast path remains a compile-time constant.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses six pre-existing bug fixes in the userfaultfd and pagemap code, which were previously carried at the front of a larger working-set-tracking series. The patches fix issues related to huge page updates, hugetlb self-deadlocks, and incorrect handling of uffd-wp markers.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v5 04/18] mm: skip out-of-range bits in mk_vma_flags()
Re: [PATCH v5 08/18] mm: add VM_UFFD_RWP VMA flag
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds a new VMA flag called VM_UFFD_RWP, which is dependent on certain kernel configuration options and architecture features. The flag is related to userfaultfd write protection, and its implementation involves defining a bit for the flag and considering its use in conjunction with another patch (04/18). The goal of this patch is to enable write protection for userfaultfd operations.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes expressed concern about the proposed solution for VM_UFFD_RWP, stating that it is not workable and suggested alternative workarounds involving VMA_UFFD_RWP.

Reviewer suggested an alternative implementation using vma_test_any_mask() to check for the VM_UFFD_RWP VMA flag, acknowledging it's 'fugly' but functional
review_comments.raw_body:
Yeah I don't think that approach is workable unfortunately (see my reply
there).

But I suggest some workarounds there with VMA_UFFD_RWP.

---

With the approach suggested in 04/18 we could do this as:

	return vma_test_any_mask(vma, VMA_UFFD_RWP);

Which is a bit fugly but will work.
